original_abstract:
Deep learning (DL) is fundamentally reshaping evidence-based decision-making across a wide range of global sectors. By leveraging multiple layers of non-linear feature transformations through representation learning, deep learning effectively addresses the complexities inherent in processing massive datasets. Despite its rapid growth, the field remains relatively young, and comprehensive surveys that capture the full breadth of evolving DL architectures are scarce. This paper provides a rigorous review of state-of-the-art deep learning modeling techniques, offering a balanced perspective on their specific advantages and inherent limitations. 

Our analysis reveals that many models demonstrate highly domain-specific efficiencies and can be optimized using diverse training methodologies. Nevertheless, significant hurdles remain: training these models is often computationally expensive and time-consuming, requiring vast datasets to achieve high precision. Furthermore, DL systems are frequently vulnerable to misclassification, adversarial deception, and the tendency of optimization algorithms to become trapped in local minima—challenges that necessitate more robust parameter optimization strategies. Despite these obstacles, deep learning has already achieved transformative results in healthcare, security, commerce, and government operations. While architectures such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are widely utilized, others remain significantly under-explored. This study highlights the potential of hybrid architectures to overcome the shortcomings of traditional models and suggests that capsule networks may represent the next frontier in the field. Ultimately, this work serves as a critical resource for stakeholders navigating the development and implementation of deep learning in the modern era.
humanized_abstract:
Deep learning (DL) is having a profound impact on many fields of global activity. The ability of deep learning to learn at multiple layers using non-linear transformations of features via representation learning makes it highly applicable to many fields. Many deep learning techniques have been proposed in the recent literature but the field is still in its infancy and comparative reviews which summarise the current state of art in the various deep learning models are not yet available in adequate measure. This paper provides a review of the various deep learning models which are currently being used with pros and cons of each.

Deep learning (DL) models such as deep neural networks and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been widely applied in many areas of interest. This work investigates how effective these models are, in terms of their applicability and applicableness, for various applications, by studying different learning algorithms in order to optimize the performance of different models for different applications. It also explores the problems of the computing cost of the training process, large training datasets required to achieve high accuracy and the related issues of biased and inaccurate classifications, as well as the adversarial attacks, and getting stuck in the local optima, which are all associated with the parameter optimization process. The applications of deep learning (DL) technology have been proved to be highly efficient and have been already applied in the medical and health care industry, national security, e-commerce and national government services. Some popular deep learning architectures such as the CNNs, RNNs and GANs are widely applied in the DL-based applications, while other DL models are not explored fully. Hence, this work investigated the potential applications of hybrid DL models, by exploring their applicability and applicableness for various applications, and identifying potential new architectures to replace traditional models, such as the capsule networks. This work provides a valuable resource to the various stakeholders that participate in the design and implementation of DL-based applications.
